text: The surgeon places trocars in the abdominal wall, preparing for laparoscopic access to the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic robotic-assisted Heller myotomy with Thor fundoplication after confirming asthenosis at the gastroesophageal junction through intraoperative endoscopy.

The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the hiatus, revealing lax adhesions between the liver, stomach, and the previous wrap. A hiatal hernia is observed, with the Nissen fundoplication partially slipped into the chest.
The surgeon performs a gentle circumferential dissection of the hiatus, releasing adhesions between the stomach, diaphragm, and caudate lobe of the liver.
The surgeon divides some of the short gastric vessels and creates a retrogastric window, allowing the passage of a Penrose drain to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon performs a dissection of the mediastinum, identifying the esophagus and continuing with a circumferential dissection of the esophagus extending at least fifteen centimeters into the chest cavity.
The surgeon repositions the Nissen funduplication and the gastroesophageal junction back into the abdominal cavity. The funduplication is carefully taken down, reversing the previous wrap.
An intraoperative endoscopy reveals a significantly dilated esophagus and a tight esophagogastric junction, visually compatible with a condition resembling achalasia.
A 48-tapered bougie is introduced into the esophagus to guide the myotomy. The surgeon performs the myotomy using electrocautery and monopolar scissors, extending five centimeters into the chest.
The surgeon transects both the longitudinal and circular muscle layers of the esophagus, continuing the dissection within the mediastinum.
The surgeon transects the transverse oblique and circular muscle layers at the gastroesophageal junction and gastric portion of the myotomy, measuring approximately three centimeters.
The surgeon carefully preserves the Bauhaus nerve during the procedure.
An intraoperative endoscopy shows no evidence of leaks, and the GE junction is traversed smoothly.
The surgeon approximates the pillars of the hiatus using interrupted stitches to restore the anatomical structure.
The surgeon places several stitches between the left and right grooves on the same side of the myotomy to keep it open.
The surgeon flips the stomach over the myotomy using the door technique, visually repositioning the stomach to cover the myotomy site.
The surgeon completes the Dor fundoplication and checks for hemostasis, ensuring there is no active bleeding in the surgical area.
